body:
First, it's refreshing to read a well thought and balanced post on Reddit.

Second, I truly believe that we can have our cake and eat it too.

"Build the guardrails. Do not kill the technology." This is the balanced approach.

All of that being said, we know that meta will do anything for shareholder value, no matter what damage it causes to society. Thus, as a society we have the right to oppose anything that will cause harm while enabling things that are beneficial to a better quality of life.

Do we really need another device that will be used to invade our privacy and disturb the rare tranquil moments in our life? No. But this should not be the reason to deprive individuals from the availability of tech that will improve their lives. My initial thought is that the mass consumer version of any smart glasses should have no capabilities to use facial recognition and the cameras covered until they are in use, while disabled or impaired consumers should have facial recognition as an option and the cameras covered until they are in use; explicit external cues to camera use are a must. Same hardware with baked in software guardrails.

My controversial take is that no business or citizen should be obliged to allow smart glasses on their property, except for disabled or impaired individuals in a similar way that access is permitted to service animals or ramps for wheel chair access is required.

Why the divide? Look at the current administration, that's it. We thought it was never going to get this bad but here we are. If they have another surface of attack they will use it against us.

body:
It’s a shame for people with disability like you highlight, but I don’t think we can trust the companies making these products. They are not making the guard rails. The fact Samsung’s latest 2026 event highlighted the ability to change people clothes using AI even after the entire Grok bikini switch incident is just totally tone-deaf. My partner’s trade school is currently dealing with a peeping tom taking photos of women as they use the bathroom, and Meta’s glasses have only made this kind of incident worse. Facebook as a company was started as a way to rate women’s looks without their knowledge. I just don’t think we can have these companies deliver safe products responsibly. They have shown time and time again they cannot. 

I’m sorry if that means accessibility progress is slowed down but **it’s not worth the risk of abuse to the population.**

For your sake, I hope a small, genuine accessibility-focused company releases safe products because I for one do not want society to be shaped by these products if they are manufactured and controlled by big tech.

Meta should be ashamed by their launch strategy, exploiting disability access… but Meta has no shame.

body:
I don’t think the backlash will amount to anything. It’s over and done. Meta’s already sold I think it’s 7M glasses, multiple orders of magnitude more than Google Glass. They’re stylish, and getting even more so, so they’re not the obvious thing that Glass was. (Which never allowed facial recognition apps - pretty much the only thing I wanted them for since I have pretty significant face blindness.)

This is basically the equivalent of people finding out that Browser Cookies can be used to track you online. People will be upset about it and that concern will largely go away.

However, I would not want to be humiliated by wearing a brightly glowing set of glasses letting everyone know that I am visually disabled.

body:
the "not everyone" framing is so dishonest. Meta has shadow profiles on people who have never made an account. they scrape contact lists, photos friends upload, location data from apps that use their SDK. so even if you've never touched facebook in your life, they probably have enough on you to build a face profile.

"we won't identify everyone you see" just means "we won't show you the results for everyone you see." the identification is still happening on their end. the data still gets collected and stored. they're just choosing what to surface to the user vs what they keep for themselves and their ad partners.

body:
Being able to take pictures, record and scan people's faces without them knowing is going to become increasingly easy until it is going to be impossible to stop. Today it is smart glasses, in 5-10 years, it is going to be BCI chips.


We need to stop focusing on the tech itself and start focusing on the companies. We need better privacy _laws_ and regulations about how companies process this data. Because you are never going to stop an individual from recording something for personal use. We just need to stop them from uploading it somewhere and that place accepting it.
